exercises.title: Swap the LM head for a classification head
exercises.prompt: To classify (spam vs ham), rip off the LM head and glue a small classification head onto the pooled representation — without re-initializing the body. Build the head swap: keep the transformer body, replace the vocab-size head with a 2-class linear layer on the pooled (last-token or mean) representation, and show a classification logit produced. Explain why the body is reused.
exercises.hint: Body produces hidden states; pool (e.g. last token) -> a vector; new head = 2-class linear on that vector. The body's pretrained weights are kept (not re-init). Show pooled vector -> 2 class logits. Reuse body: it already learned language; only the head needs to be new.
exercises.solution:
Classifier fine-tuning keeps the pretrained body and swaps only the head — the body already understands language.

```python
def classification_head(pooled_vector, head_weights):
    # 2-class linear on the pooled representation
    return [sum(p*w for p, w in zip(pooled_vector, head_weights[c])) for c in range(2)]

def pool(hidden_states, strategy='last'):
    return hidden_states[-1] if strategy == 'last' else \
           [sum(h[i] for h in hidden_states)/len(hidden_states) for i in range(len(hidden_states[0]))]

# body (pretrained, REUSED) produces hidden states; new head classifies
hidden_states = [[1.0, 0.5], [0.8, 0.9], [0.2, 1.0]]
pooled = pool(hidden_states, 'last')
head_weights = [[1.0, 0.0], [0.0, 1.0]]   # the only NEW parameters
logits = classification_head(pooled, head_weights)
print('pooled:', pooled)
print('class logits (ham, spam):', logits)
```

Output:

```
pooled: [0.2, 1.0]
class logits (ham, spam): [0.2, 1.0]
```

**Why reuse the body.** A pretrained model is a stack of attention blocks that already learned rich language representations — for spam-vs-ham, the *body is mostly right*, only the *head is wrong* (it predicts vocabulary tokens, not classes). The head swap keeps the body's pretrained weights (no re-initialization) and glues a small 2-class linear layer onto the *pooled* representation (last token or mean). This reuses millions of parameters of language understanding and learns only the tiny new head — far more efficient than training a classifier from scratch. The next exercises compare *which* parameters to train.

tests.desc: last-token pooling takes the final state
the head produces a logit per class

exercises.title: Final-layer-only vs full fine-tuning
exercises.prompt: Two strategies: train only the final layer + head (cheap, fast) or full fine-tuning (all params, expensive). Build a parameter-count and a simulated accuracy comparison, showing final-layer-only training far fewer parameters at somewhat lower accuracy, full fine-tuning more parameters at higher accuracy. Discuss what each buys and costs.
exercises.hint: final-layer-only: trainable params = last block + head (small). full: all params (large). Simulate: final-only reaches lower accuracy faster/cheaper; full reaches higher accuracy at more cost. Show param counts + accuracy. Tradeoff: final-only is cheap but caps accuracy; full is expensive but best.
exercises.solution:
Two fine-tuning strategies trade trainable parameters (cost) against accuracy.

```python
def strategy_profile(strategy, total_params=124_000_000):
    if strategy == 'final_layer_only':
        trainable = int(total_params * 0.06)    # last block + head
        accuracy = 0.91
    else:  # full fine-tuning
        trainable = total_params
        accuracy = 0.96
    return trainable, accuracy

for s in ('final_layer_only', 'full'):
    trainable, acc = strategy_profile(s)
    print(f"{s:>18}: {trainable:>12,} trainable params, accuracy {acc:.0%}")
```

Output:

```
  final_layer_only:    7,440,000 trainable params, accuracy 91%
              full: 124,000,000 trainable params, accuracy 96%
```

**What each strategy buys.** *Final-layer-only* trains just the last block + head (~6% of parameters) — *cheap and fast* (less memory, fewer gradients, lower risk of overfitting on small data), but the frozen body limits how much the model can adapt, *capping accuracy* (91%). *Full fine-tuning* trains all parameters — *expensive* (full backward pass, more memory, more overfitting risk on small data) but lets the whole model adapt to the task, reaching *higher accuracy* (96%). The choice depends on your data size and compute: small labeled set or tight budget → final-layer-only; ample data and compute → full. The final exercise evaluates them properly.
exercises.starter_code:
def strategy_profile(strategy, total_params=124000000):
    # 'final_layer_only' -> ~6% trainable, 0.91 acc; 'full' -> all params, 0.96.
    # Return (trainable, accuracy).
    pass

exercises.title: Precision, recall, and F1 on a held-out split
exercises.prompt: The eval is precision, recall, and F1 on a held-out split — accuracy alone misleads on imbalanced classes. Build the metric computation from predictions and labels, and show that on an imbalanced spam dataset, accuracy looks high but recall reveals missed spam. Discuss why F1 (not accuracy) is the right metric for classification.
exercises.hint: precision = TP/(TP+FP), recall = TP/(TP+FN), F1 = harmonic mean. On imbalanced data (mostly ham), a model that predicts all-ham has high accuracy but zero recall on spam. Show accuracy high, recall low. F1 balances precision+recall, exposing the failure.
exercises.solution:
Classification eval uses precision, recall, and F1 — accuracy alone hides failures on imbalanced classes.

```python
def metrics(preds, labels, positive=1):
    tp = sum(p == positive and l == positive for p, l in zip(preds, labels))
    fp = sum(p == positive and l != positive for p, l in zip(preds, labels))
    fn = sum(p != positive and l == positive for p, l in zip(preds, labels))
    acc = sum(p == l for p, l in zip(preds, labels)) / len(labels)
    prec = tp / (tp + fp) if tp + fp else 0
    rec = tp / (tp + fn) if tp + fn else 0
    f1 = 2*prec*rec / (prec + rec) if prec + rec else 0
    return {'accuracy': acc, 'precision': prec, 'recall': rec, 'f1': f1}

# imbalanced: 90 ham, 10 spam. Model predicts ALL ham.
labels = [0]*90 + [1]*10
all_ham = [0]*100
print('predict-all-ham:', {k: round(v, 2) for k, v in metrics(all_ham, labels).items()})
```

Output:

```
predict-all-ham: {'accuracy': 0.9, 'precision': 0, 'recall': 0.0, 'f1': 0}
```

**Why F1, not accuracy.** On an imbalanced dataset (90 ham, 10 spam), a model that predicts *everything is ham* scores *90% accuracy* — looking great — while catching *zero spam* (recall 0, F1 0). Accuracy is dominated by the majority class and *hides the complete failure* on the minority class (spam), which is exactly the class you care about. *Recall* exposes the missed spam; *precision* would expose false alarms; *F1* (their harmonic mean) summarizes both, going to 0 here to flag the useless model. For classification — especially imbalanced — F1 on a held-out split is the honest metric.

**The classifier-finetuning synthesis.** Swap the LM head for a classification head while reusing the pretrained body (Ex. 1), choose final-layer-only (cheap, accuracy-capped) or full fine-tuning (expensive, higher accuracy) by your data and budget (Ex. 2), and evaluate with precision/recall/F1 on a held-out split — not accuracy, which lies on imbalanced data (Ex. 3). Track B's first capstone: "the head is wrong but the body is mostly right" — fine-tune by head swap, and measure honestly. The next lessons fine-tune for generation (SFT, DPO).

tests.desc: predicting all-majority misses the minority class
